titleStmt.title: Letter from Susan Fitzgerald to Michael Gorman, 23 May 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Susan Fitzgerald

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Susan Fitzgerald (d. 1980) to Michael Gorman. The letter was written from Susan's home and she mentions minding some of family's children, a cold she picked up in Dublin, a photograph he had sent, the weather and a plan to meet with Michael. She also refers to an exam in London that Michael was unable to travel to.Michael Gorman was one a large Catholic family living at a farm in Ballinalug, County Laois (then Queen's County). Susan Fitzgerald was one a Church of Ireland family living in Raheenahone House near Stradbally, County Laois, also in the Slieve Bloom mountains. At the time the letter was written they were involved in a courtship but owing to the religious difference the love affair had to be kept secret. Eventually both were in Dublin: he in the Albert Agricultural College in Glasnevin (where he eventually became Professor of Agricultural Botany and Biochemistry) and she as a volunteer nurse in the Royal Hospital in Baggot Street. They eventually went to London to marry.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Writing at the top of the page written sideways I quite forgot to
bring the pink shoes when coming
home. Will you please
let me have them.
I hope it isnt too much trouble
Love
Sue Sunday Beginning of letter Raheenahone House Stradbally Queens Co My dearest Mickie I have been
threatening to write
to you all last week
and I made several
attempts on yesterday
but didn't succeed.
Mother & Fannie went
to Dublin for the day
& I was asked to
keep an eye on the
write very soon a long letter kids, so I had a very
busy time trying to keep
them out of mischief.
They are very wild, but
most delightful, especially
the boy. We expect
to have them here another
week, & I'll be sorry
when they leave. I
got a bad cold coming
home from Dublin, &
I haven't been outside
the house since, but my
throat is nearly all
right again.
I was sorry
to hear you where are
unable to get across
to London for your exam
this time. It might be
just as well, especially
when you had such
a short time to work
for it; and as you say,
if you go up next year
you'll have far more
confidence. Thanks for the latest
style in photography
It's simply too funny!
M.R. is splendid also
[So of hits] aren't we having
glorious weather. I hope
June will be as nice,
particularly the first
week-end. I may be
able to make some
arrangement to see you
in Maryboro' & we can
talk about your holiday
week-ends etc then. I
suppose Maryboro' is the
best place. You see I'll
have to drive. Im not
able to walk or cycle
yet.I'm taking a long
time to get really well.
Did you hear from McAuley
since? I replied to his letter
at last. Indeed I owe several
letters & I'm leading such
an idle life.It makes me
quite lazy sitting out most of
the day reading & darning
at present I'm making a very pretty
costume.I hope it isn't a failure
when finished. Did you get your new suit Heaps of love from Sue.
Mr. J. Gorman Esq. Albert College Glasnevin Dubin
